content.assessment: This is the most legally demanding proposal: it requires parliamentary amendment to primary legislation and direct confrontation with a constitutionally protected right to free choice of institution, creating an unresolved rights-compatibility problem. It also requires clarifying which authority locus, Parliament, Klebelsberg Centre, or church/private maintainers, would control implementation, since current governance is split between centralized operational control and constitutionally protected choice.
opportunities: A parliamentary process, while a high bar, would at least produce a durable and unambiguous legal instrument rather than a contestable administrative order, potentially resolving the authority-locus ambiguity identified across the system (F-live-legal_and_governance-10)
strengths: Correctly identifies that structural delay requires parliamentary amendment rather than administrative action, matching the codification of the 6/8-year tracks in primary legislation (F-live-legal_and_governance-01)

Explicitly recognizes that anti-sorting catchment rules must be bundled with the age change to have legal traction given the constitutional choice guarantee, showing awareness of the core legal design tension (F-live-legal_and_governance-02)
threats: The multiplicity of authority loci (Parliament, Klebelsberg Centre, individual maintainers, church/private entities) with no single accountable implementer creates a serious risk of fragmented or contested implementation even after enactment (F-live-legal_and_governance-10)

Extreme existing selectivity and segregation levels indicate the governance system currently tolerates high inequality outcomes despite constitutional guarantees, suggesting judicial or political resistance to change is plausible (F-live-legal_and_governance-04, F-live-legal_and_governance-06)
weaknesses: The proposal does not resolve, only names, the constitutional tension: restricting selection without restricting choice is not straightforward under current doctrine, and no concrete legal mechanism is offered for reconciling the two (F-live-legal_and_governance-02)

Church and private schools fall outside state maintainer jurisdiction, so a uniform delay enacted via state legislation may not bind these providers, undermining enforceability of a supposedly uniform reform (F-live-legal_and_governance-07)
